title: Torus Surface Area Calculator
tagline: Calculate the total surface area of a torus using its major and minor radii instantly.
intro: Enter the major radius (R) and minor radius (r) of the torus, then click Calculate to get the surface area.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for both radii.
error.minorExceedsMajor: The minor radius (r) cannot be greater than the major radius (R).
error.zero: Radii must be greater than zero.

about.heading: About the Torus Surface Area Calculator
about.body:
A torus is the three-dimensional shape you get when you revolve a circle around an axis that lies in the same plane as the circle but does not intersect it. The resulting form resembles a donut, a ring, an O-ring, or an inflated inner tube. Because the torus combines circular curvature in two independent directions — around the central axis and around the tube itself — its surface area formula is elegantly compact.

The surface area of a torus is given by SA = 4π²Rr, where R is the major radius (the distance from the center of the torus to the center of the tube) and r is the minor radius (the radius of the tube itself). You can also write this as SA = (2πR)(2πr), which makes clear that the surface area equals the circumference of the path the center of the tube travels multiplied by the circumference of the tube's cross-section. This is a beautiful result from Pappus's centroid theorem: the surface area generated by revolving a curve is equal to the length of the curve times the distance traveled by its centroid.

In practical applications you encounter tori everywhere. Engineers calculate the surface area of O-rings to determine how much sealing compound or lubricant to apply. Architects use toroidal shapes for curved structural members and need surface area for cladding and coating estimates. Industrial designers compute the surface area of tori when specifying paint, plating, or thermal insulation for ring-shaped components such as pipe flanges, gaskets, and decorative bezels. Educators use the torus as a teaching example because it illustrates how a simple rotation can produce a complex-looking shape with a surprisingly clean formula.

The calculator is valid for a standard ring torus, where r < R, which means the hole in the middle is real. If r = R the torus degenerates into a horn torus (the hole closes to a point), and if r > R it becomes a spindle torus (the surfaces self-intersect). For the horn and spindle cases the formula SA = 4π²Rr still gives the correct mathematical surface area, but the physical interpretation changes. This calculator works for all positive R and r values, so you can freely explore degenerate cases.

All inputs are dimensionless, so the units of the output match the units you use for input: if you enter centimeters the result is in square centimeters; if you enter inches the result is in square inches. For very large or very small values the calculator displays up to ten significant digits to maintain accuracy across engineering scales.

examples.heading: Torus surface area examples
examples.intro: Four worked examples showing the formula applied to common real-world objects.

items.note: SA = 4π² × 10 × 2 = 80π² ≈ 789.57. A medium-sized ring shape typical of jewelry molds or gaskets.
SA = 4π² × 25 × 8 = 800π² ≈ 7,896.0. Represents a small vehicle inner tube; useful for rubber coating estimates.
SA = 4π² × 50 × 5 = 1000π² ≈ 9,869.6. A large thin toroidal facade element; surface area drives cladding cost.
SA = 4π² × 4 × 1.5 = 24π² ≈ 236.87. A typical sealing O-ring; surface area determines lubricant volume needed.

howto.heading: How to use the Torus Surface Area Calculator
steps: Measure or note the major radius R — the distance from the center of the torus to the center of the tube.
Measure or note the minor radius r — the radius of the circular cross-section of the tube itself.
Enter both values in the corresponding input fields. Ensure R ≥ r for a standard ring torus.
Click Calculate Surface Area. The result appears immediately in square units matching your input units.
Click Reset to clear all fields and perform a new calculation.

faq.heading: Torus Surface Area Calculator FAQ
faq.q1: What is the formula for the surface area of a torus?
faq.q1_a: The formula is SA = 4π²Rr, where R is the major radius (center of torus to center of tube) and r is the minor radius (radius of the tube). Equivalently, SA = (2πR)(2πr), which is the product of the two circumferences. This result follows from Pappus's centroid theorem.
faq.q2: What is the difference between the major radius and the minor radius?
faq.q2_a: The major radius R is measured from the central axis of the torus to the center of the circular tube. The minor radius r is the radius of that tube itself. Think of R as how wide the ring is and r as how thick the tube is. For a typical donut, R is roughly the distance from the center hole to the middle of the dough, and r is roughly half the dough's thickness.
faq.q3: Can the minor radius be larger than the major radius?
faq.q3_a: Mathematically yes, and the formula SA = 4π²Rr still applies, but the resulting shape is a spindle torus whose outer surfaces self-intersect. In engineering applications this configuration is physically impossible for a hollow tube, so most real-world calculations require r ≤ R.
faq.q4: What units does the calculator use?
faq.q4_a: The calculator is unit-agnostic. Enter your measurements in any consistent unit (meters, centimeters, inches, feet), and the result will be in that unit squared. If you enter R = 10 cm and r = 2 cm, the result is in square centimeters.
faq.q5: How is this different from the volume of a torus?
faq.q5_a: Surface area (SA = 4π²Rr) measures the two-dimensional area of the outer skin of the torus, useful for coating, painting, or plating calculations. Volume (V = 2π²Rr²) measures the three-dimensional interior space, useful for capacity or mass calculations. Both formulas share the same Pappus-theorem derivation.
faq.q6: Where is torus surface area used in engineering?
faq.q6_a: Common applications include: sizing the amount of rubber compound or lubricant for O-rings and seals, calculating the metal or composite area of toroidal pressure vessels and fuel tanks, estimating coating material for ring-shaped machine components, and computing the surface area of architectural toroidal structures for cladding and insulation. In each case, surface area drives material cost and process time, making an accurate calculator essential.
